skill.name: nature-data
skill.description: Prepare, audit, or revise Nature-ready Data Availability statements, data repository plans, dataset citations, and FAIR metadata checklists for manuscripts.
skill.summary: Draft Nature journal Data Availability statements with repository selection, FAIR metadata, and bilingual Chinese-English support.
skill.author: Yuan1z skill, refactored into static/dynamic layers

security_audit.summary: This is a pure documentation and writing-assistance skill with no executable code. All 125 static findings are false positives triggered by markdown syntax: backticks in prose (misread as shell execution), mentions of data 'hash' or 'SHA' in FAIR metadata context (misread as weak crypto), and links to legitimate academic repositories like DataCite (misread as hardcoded network endpoints). No actual code execution, network calls, file system access, or cryptographic operations exist in this skill.

content.value_statement: Researchers preparing Nature submissions struggle to produce compliant Data Availability statements. This skill provides structured guidance for repository selection, FAIR metadata, and bilingual wording.
actual_capabilities: Draft Data Availability statements matching Nature journal requirements
Classify datasets into access routes (public, controlled, within paper, third-party)
Recommend appropriate repositories and identifier strategies (DOI, accession numbers)
Generate FAIR metadata checklists for dataset submissions
Convert Chinese-language data notes into publication-ready English statements
Provide ready-to-adapt statement patterns for common data scenarios
limitations: Cannot verify or validate actual DOIs, accession numbers, or repository availability
Does not check journal-specific requirements beyond Nature family guidelines
Cannot access external repositories or fetch live metadata
Provides templates and guidance, not legal or ethical compliance certification

use_cases.title: Prepare a Nature submission
use_cases.description: Draft complete Data Availability statements and repository plans for a Nature-family manuscript submission.
use_cases.target_user: Academic researchers submitting to Nature journals

use_cases.title: FAIR metadata audit
use_cases.description: Review and improve dataset metadata to meet FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) standards.

use_cases.title: Chinese-English bilingual drafting
use_cases.description: Convert Chinese-language research notes and data descriptions into Nature-compliant English availability statements.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a basic Data Availability statement
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write a Data Availability statement for my Nature submission. My study generated RNA-seq data from 24 samples and uses a previously published reference dataset.

prompt_templates.title: Repository and identifier selection
prompt_templates.prompt: I have three datasets: human genomic data (controlled access required), environmental metagenomics (public), and a custom analysis pipeline (code only). Recommend repositories and identifier types for each.

prompt_templates.title: FAIR metadata checklist review
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit my dataset metadata against FAIR principles. Here are my current README contents and DataCite fields. Identify gaps and suggest improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Bilingual Chinese-English conversion
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this Chinese data availability note into a Nature-compliant English statement. The Chinese text describes our single-cell sequencing data deposited in GSA with restricted access for ethical reasons.

output_examples.input: We generated RNA-seq data from 24 mouse liver samples. Raw and processed data are available.
output: Raw and processed RNA-seq data from 24 mouse liver samples have been deposited in the Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) under accession number GSEXXXXXX.
All analysis scripts are available at https://github.com/author/repository under MIT license.
Any additional information is available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

output_examples.input: I have human patient genomic data that needs controlled access due to privacy regulations.
output: Human genomic data supporting this study are available under controlled access from the European Genome-phenome Archive (EGA) at accession EGADXXXXXXX. Access is granted to researchers who comply with the data use agreement. Applications should be directed to the relevant Data Access Committee.

best_practices: Always classify each dataset into a specific access route before drafting the statement
Include formal dataset citations with DOIs or accession numbers, not just repository names
Flag 'available upon request' as a weak statement unless a specific restriction applies
Verify repository suitability for your data type before recommending it
anti_patterns: Inventing DOIs, accession numbers, or repository names that do not exist
Using 'data are available upon reasonable request' without justification for the restriction
Omitting dataset citations when datasets have been deposited in public repositories
Applying generic statements without classifying individual dataset access requirements

faq.question: What is a Data Availability statement?
faq.answer: A Data Availability statement describes where readers can access the data supporting a research paper. Nature journals require specific formats depending on the data type and access conditions.

faq.question: Which repository should I use for my data?
faq.answer: Repository choice depends on data type. Use domain-specific repositories (GEO for gene expression, PDB for protein structures) when available. Use general repositories like Zenodo or Figshare for other data types.

faq.question: Can I say 'data available upon request'?
faq.answer: Nature considers this a weak statement. You can use it only when a specific legal, ethical, commercial, or third-party restriction prevents public deposition. Always state the restriction.

faq.question: What is FAIR metadata?
faq.answer: FAIR stands for Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable. FAIR metadata standards ensure datasets can be discovered, accessed, combined with other data, and reused by future researchers.

faq.question: Does this skill support Chinese-language input?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill accepts Chinese-language research notes and drafts Nature-compliant English Data Availability statements. It includes a dedicated Chinese-author alignment reference for bilingual accuracy.

faq.question: Can this skill verify my DOIs or accession numbers?
faq.answer: No. The skill provides guidance and templates but cannot verify external identifiers. Always confirm DOIs and accession numbers through the actual repository before submission.
